# AI-Use Declaration — Manual Review Checklist

**Status as of Phase 7C.** Generated by the Springer compliance audit system.
Last regenerated: see git log for this file.

This document exists because `springer-ai-declaration-audit` flags a **MANUAL**
item on every run: *"Declaration present — confirm wording matches Springer
policy."* The skill can detect that a declaration is *present*; it cannot verify
that the *wording* is current. That is a human task, repeated whenever Springer
updates its editorial policy.

---

## 1. Current declaration text (verbatim, committed in repo)

Source: `author/acknowledgement.tex`, lines 29–53 (as of commit where this
review file is committed).

```latex
\section*{Declaration of AI-Assisted Content}

In accordance with Springer Nature's policy on the use of large language
models in authored content, I disclose the following:

Portions of this manuscript were developed with the assistance of large
language model tools, specifically Anthropic Claude
Opus 4.7, used in the following capacities:

\begin{itemize}
  \item Drafting and revision of prose, with all substantive content
        reviewed, verified, and edited by the author.
  \item Generation of illustrative code listings and LaTeX source for
        figures typeset via TikZ and PGFPlots. All figures in this
        book are vector drawings; no figures were produced using
        generative-AI image synthesis.
  \item Structural compliance auditing of the manuscript against
        Springer Nature's monograph formatting requirements.
\end{itemize}

The author retains full responsibility for the accuracy, originality,
and integrity of all content in this book. No AI tool is listed as an
author, and no AI-generated content was incorporated without author
review. Citations, technical claims, and attributions have been
verified by the author against primary sources.
```

---

## 2. Springer Nature policy — canonical source of truth

**Do not rely on this document for policy language.** Fetch the current
version from Springer before submission and diff against the text above.

Primary references (consult in this order):

1. **Springer Nature editorial policies index**
   <https://www.springernature.com/gp/policies/editorial-policies>
2. **AI policy for books and chapters** (specific sub-page or section titled
   similarly to *"Use of Generative AI in books"* or *"Artificial
   intelligence"*). The URL and exact wording have changed in the past; always
   locate by policy name, not by bookmarked URL.
3. **Author submission guidelines for monographs** in the Springer Author
   Academy, which may echo or expand the core policy page with examples of
   acceptable declaration wording.

---

## 3. Review checklist — each item must PASS before submission

Work through these by comparing the committed declaration (§1) to the current
Springer policy (§2). Mark each row.

| # | Required element | Present in our text? | Matches current policy wording? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Explicit statement that the manuscript was developed with AI assistance | ✅ Yes — "Portions of this manuscript were developed with the assistance of large language model tools" | ☐ Verify |
| 2 | Named LLM tool(s) with vendor and version | ✅ Yes — "Anthropic Claude Opus 4.7" | ☐ Verify version string matches what was actually used for substantive content |
| 3 | Scope of AI use enumerated (drafting / code / analysis / etc.) | ✅ Yes — three bullets: prose drafting, TikZ listings, compliance auditing | ☐ Verify that Springer still accepts this level of granularity and that each listed use is a *permitted* use per current policy |
| 4 | Explicit statement that author reviewed and edited all AI-assisted content | ✅ Yes — "all substantive content reviewed, verified, and edited by the author" | ☐ Verify |
| 5 | Explicit statement that author retains full responsibility | ✅ Yes — "The author retains full responsibility for the accuracy, originality, and integrity of all content" | ☐ Verify |
| 6 | Explicit statement that no AI is listed as an author | ✅ Yes — "No AI tool is listed as an author" | ☐ Verify |
| 7 | Explicit statement that no generative-AI *figures* (images) were used | ✅ Yes — "no figures were produced using generative-AI image synthesis" | ☐ Verify; Springer has been strict about this. Figures produced via deterministic code (TikZ / PGFPlots / matplotlib / ggplot2) are generally considered non-generative even if the LaTeX/code was assisted. |
| 8 | Declaration placed in a location Springer expects (acknowledgements, preface, methods, or dedicated section) | ✅ Yes — inside Acknowledgements chapter as a dedicated `\section*{Declaration of AI-Assisted Content}` | ☐ Verify Springer's preferred location for monographs (some policies prefer the Preface; others accept Acknowledgements) |
| 9 | Citations were verified by the author against primary sources | ✅ Yes — "Citations, technical claims, and attributions have been verified by the author against primary sources" | ☐ Verify; this addresses a specific Springer concern about AI-fabricated citations |
| 10 | Language matches Springer's exact verbiage where they prescribe it | ☐ Needs verification | ☐ Some policies suggest ("we recommend the following wording") but do not mandate; others mandate. Verify current status. |

**All ☐ cells must be ticked (verified) before the manuscript is submitted.**

---

## 4. Known Springer concerns to double-check

Springer policy updates over recent years have emphasized, in rough order:

1. **No AI authorship.** LLMs cannot be listed as co-authors (this is settled
   policy across most major publishers; present in our §1).
2. **No generative-AI figures.** Images generated by text-to-image models
   (Midjourney, DALL·E, Stable Diffusion, etc.) are not accepted as figures in
   scientific/technical works except narrowly for works *about* generative AI.
   Our declaration addresses this explicitly. Figures typeset from code
   (TikZ, matplotlib, etc.) are not considered "generative-AI figures" even if
   the code was AI-assisted.
3. **Transparency over granularity.** The declaration should be honest about
   scope but does not require an exhaustive per-paragraph provenance trail.
4. **Responsibility cannot be outsourced.** Every factual claim, citation, and
   argument is the author's responsibility, regardless of which tool produced
   the first draft.
5. **Citation verification.** AI-fabricated citations (hallucinated refs) are a
   known failure mode; the declaration should affirm verification.

Our §1 text addresses each of these. Verify the wording is still current.

---

## 5. How to refresh this review

When Springer publishes a new version of the policy (or before any major
milestone like proofs or final submission):

```bash
# 1. Fetch the current policy page. Read the AI-use section.
# 2. Diff §1 (this file) against the policy wording.
# 3. If wording has drifted, edit author/acknowledgement.tex accordingly.
# 4. Rebuild and run the audit:
make build
make audit
# 5. Re-tick the ☐ boxes in §3 with the new policy version noted in prose.
# 6. Commit this file as: docs(review): refresh AI-declaration checklist
#    against Springer policy rev YYYY-MM-DD
```

---

## 6. Audit-system integration

This document satisfies the MANUAL item flagged by
`springer-ai-declaration-audit`:

```
- Declaration present — **MANUAL REVIEW**: confirm wording matches Springer policy
```

The audit will continue to emit the MANUAL flag on every run — that is
intentional. A MANUAL flag is not a failure; it is a reminder that one human
check cannot be automated. This file is the living evidence of that check.
